Vancomycin is added when MRSA risk is high or central line associated, per RCH CPG: Sepsis and Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections.
key_points: Recent oropharyngeal lesion is a hook for Kingella kingae - it enters through breached oral mucosa
Australian paediatric sepsis pathway: 10 mL/kg crystalloid bolus, reassess, repeat as needed
Source control (washout) is as important as antibiotics in septic arthritis
Add vancomycin for MRSA risk (e.g. remote Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities, line-associated, severe systemic illness)
explanation.pearls: Bacteraemia precedes joint seeding; always draw blood cultures with the first peripheral cannula before antibiotics where it does not delay therapy (Source: RCH CPG: Sepsis).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Bone and joint infections

subtopic_detail: Septic arthritis in unwell child - resuscitation and source control

stem: A 9-year-old Aboriginal boy from a remote community presents to the local clinic with a 10 day history of migratory arthritis affecting his right knee, then left ankle and now both wrists. The pain is severe enough to limit weight-bearing. He recalls a sore throat 3 weeks ago that resolved without medical review. He has not had any rash, no nodules and no chorea. On examination he has warm, tender, swollen wrists bilaterally with limited range of movement; the knee and ankle are no longer involved. There is no rash, no subcutaneous nodules and no abnormal involuntary movements. Cardiovascular examination reveals a soft apical pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla that has not previously been documented. Investigations are back. The clinic team asks whether this could be post-streptococcal reactive arthritis, JIA, HSP or Lyme arthritis given the migratory pattern and remote setting.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.2 deg C, HR 124 bpm, RR 22 /min, BP 102/62 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Bilateral warm tender swollen wrists; cardiac apical pansystolic murmur radiating to axilla; no rash; no nodules; no chorea
data_table.Investigations: WCC 11.6, CRP 78 mg/L, ESR 64 mm/h, ASOT 850 IU/mL (elevated), anti-DNase B elevated, echocardiogram shows mild mitral regurgitation with no chamber dilatation
data_table.Past: No previous documented ARF, fully immunised, no penicillin allergy

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis affecting multiple joints in additive pattern without convincing evidence of carditis on echocardiogram in this Aboriginal child from a remote community
options.rationale: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis lacks evidence of carditis and has a more prolonged additive arthritis pattern; this child has objective carditis on echo and migratory polyarthritis, which fulfils ARF criteria in a high-risk population, per RHDAustralia 2020 guideline.

options.text: Acute rheumatic fever fulfilling Australian modified Jones criteria for a high-risk population with carditis and migratory polyarthritis as a definite first episode in this Aboriginal child
options.rationale: In an Aboriginal child from a remote community (high-risk population) the modified Australian Jones criteria require 2 major or 1 major + 2 minor with evidence of preceding GAS infection. This child has 2 majors (carditis on echo and polyarthritis) plus elevated inflammatory markers and ASOT, fulfilling definite ARF, per RHDAustralia 2020 guideline.

options.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis polyarticular subtype with incidental cardiac murmur and elevated streptococcal serology unrelated to the migratory arthritis presentation in this school-aged child
options.rationale: JIA typically requires 6 weeks of persistent arthritis in one or more joints and is not associated with a new pansystolic mitral murmur and elevated streptococcal serology with a sore throat history; ARF fits the picture better, per RHDAustralia guideline.

options.text: Henoch-Schonlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) presenting with arthritis as the dominant initial feature ahead of any palpable purpuric rash, abdominal pain or renal involvement
options.rationale: HSP requires palpable purpura on dependent areas plus arthritis, abdominal pain or renal involvement; this child has no rash, no abdominal pain and no haematuria, making HSP unlikely, per RCH CPG: HSP.

options.text: Lyme arthritis from Borrelia burgdorferi infection acquired locally in the remote Australian setting despite the absence of erythema migrans rash or any relevant overseas travel
options.rationale: Indigenous Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi) is not established in Australia; this child has no relevant travel history and the migratory polyarthritis with carditis and positive streptococcal serology points to ARF, per RHDAustralia guideline.

explanation.summary: Acute rheumatic fever is diagnosed by the Australian modified Jones criteria, with a lower threshold for diagnosis in high-risk populations (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, Maori and Pacific peoples). Major manifestations are carditis (subclinical detectable on echo), arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules; minors include fever, raised ESR / CRP, monoarthralgia, prolonged PR. Evidence of preceding GAS (ASOT, anti-DNase B, throat swab) is required, per RHDAustralia 2020 guideline.
key_points: High-risk: 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor + GAS evidence
Standard-risk: same plus more stringent arthritis definitions
Treat acute episode with bed rest, NSAIDs (after diagnosis), eradicate GAS
Secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine benzylpenicillin every 21-28 days until at least age 21 and 10 years from last episode
explanation.pearls: Even subclinical mitral regurgitation on echocardiogram now counts as carditis under the 2015 / 2020 Australian modified Jones criteria; always obtain echo when ARF is suspected (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).

subtopic_detail: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis versus acute rheumatic fever

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to his GP with recurrent severe right-sided nose bleeds over the past 4 months. Each episode lasts more than 20 minutes and on two occasions has required attendance at an ED for anterior nasal packing. He also reports a 3 month history of progressive right-sided nasal obstruction and intermittent right-sided cheek fullness. There is no history of trauma, no significant nose picking and no family history of bleeding disorders. He is not on any medication. He is otherwise well, growing along the 50th centile, and has not lost weight. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a pink-grey mass filling the posterior right nasal cavity that bled briskly on attempted swab. Full blood count and coagulation screen are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 84 bpm, RR 16 /min, BP 116/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Right posterior nasal pink-grey mass, brisk bleeding on touch, right-sided nasal obstruction, no cervical lymphadenopathy, no facial nerve weakness

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure as recurrent benign anterior epistaxis from Little area and prescribe topical Naseptin (chlorhexidine and neomycin) ointment twice daily for 2 weeks with first-aid advice
options.rationale: Severe recurrent unilateral epistaxis with nasal obstruction and a visible posterior mass in an adolescent male must not be treated as benign anterior epistaxis; topical antiseptic alone misses juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.

options.text: Attempt bedside biopsy of the visible posterior nasal mass with a cup forceps under topical lignocaine to confirm histology before any imaging or specialist ENT referral
options.rationale: JNA is exceptionally vascular and biopsy in the clinic can cause life-threatening haemorrhage; tissue diagnosis is made after contrast imaging in a controlled surgical environment, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis and ENT consensus.

options.text: Refer urgently to ENT and arrange contrast-enhanced MRI or CT of the nasopharynx given red flag features (recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis, progressive nasal obstruction and a visible mass in an adolescent male)
options.rationale: The triad of recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis, progressive nasal obstruction and a posterior nasal mass in an adolescent male is the classic presentation of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma; urgent ENT referral with contrast cross-sectional imaging is required and biopsy is avoided in clinic, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.

options.text: Apply silver nitrate chemical cautery to both sides of the nasal septum at Little area today, prescribe topical antibiotic ointment and arrange GP review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks septal perforation; cautery does not address a posterior nasopharyngeal mass and would not treat JNA, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.

options.text: Commence oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily for 6 weeks as definitive antifibrinolytic management for recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis with a posterior nasal mass
options.rationale: Oral tranexamic acid is an adjunct, not a substitute for definitive ENT assessment and imaging of a suspected vascular nasopharyngeal tumour, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.

explanation.summary: Most paediatric epistaxis is anterior and benign, but red flags require ENT referral. The classic triad of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis, progressive nasal obstruction and a posterior nasal mass in an adolescent male; clinic biopsy is contraindicated and management requires contrast imaging and specialist surgical (often pre-embolised) resection, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.
key_points: JNA almost exclusively occurs in adolescent males
Other red flags: bleeding from multiple sites, family history of bleeding disorder, easy bruising, bleeding after dental work
Posterior epistaxis is more common in adults / hypertensive bleeders
Investigations: FBC, coagulation, group and hold; contrast imaging for suspected mass
explanation.pearls: Adolescent boy with severe unilateral nose bleeds plus blocked nose equals JNA until proven otherwise (Source: BMJ Best Practice - JNA).

subtopic_detail: Adolescent epistaxis - red flags for juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
